title: 2/21/20 Turn comments back on for freedom FOR KIDS

transcript.text: Hi, Living in Boulder Vlog. It's like 7.45 in the morning on like the 21st. I'm getting up today like in the morning. Yeah, because I can't really sleep anymore. So that's great. I'm finally starting to be a diurnalist.
I laid in bed at like 9 yesterday. I was exercising until 8 and then had a shower and then went to the supermarket to get some juice and stuff. And then came back here and laid down at like 9.

And yeah, in order to night before I got up really late yesterday because I was up really late the night before last because I rode my bike 100 miles for the first time since being back and kind of took it out of me.
I threw I dry heaved a bunch of times. I only ate a banana. That was the only thing I ate yesterday. But after the ride, I dry heaved a bunch of times and laid back.

I had to keep running back to the, you know, toilet to dry heave. And then I was laying in my bed. I threw up my mouth. So I had to run back to the toilet and and thought I wasn't going to be able to eat anything.
But then my roommate gave me money to go get a pizza and I managed to eat that. So but then yesterday I got up kind of late and rode to, you know, like at one or something.
So I got a really late start because I had to I had insomnia from being feverish and sick from the ride.
And then and then so I had to get my catch up sleep until one and then stretch for like an hour and then finally got back, got on the something at like 430 and went until eight.
I can make noise on my exercise machines.
This is how 10 at 10 at night. That's the rules for the home of the apartments.
So but yeah, I went to eight and I managed to do 10 kilometers.
So I did 100 miles on the ride and then 10 kilometers on the swim machine.
I did the first two kilometers on level seven, which is the hardest level.
It's like lifting weights and then kilometer two to four on level six, four to six and level five, six to eight level four and then eight to ten.
And then level three and yeah, it was, it was a nice way to do it.
Um, I did like three hours, 15 minutes and that includes, um, three hours, 15 minutes.
And that includes, um, getting off, you know, every 200, 2000 meters to, you know, I had to adjust which lights I wanted in my room and, um, and get drink, get drink water.
And I had a little bit of the goo in between and, um, to, uh, replay my, uh, my, uh, book, my, I was listening to Montesquieu the whole time.
Um, so I would do, uh, three chapters at a time.
You can't do an entire book and have it read to you because after, after an hour, it gets so garbled.
You can't even hear what it's saying and then you don't know where you stopped and then you have to redo stuff.
So the technique I use is I just do what looks like an hour of speaking or almost an hour, um, like three chapters.
I do three chapters at a time and then when it stops, I just go and do the next three chapters.
Um, so yeah, and then when I went to sleep, um, I stopped, I stopped swimming at eight and then went over to the supermarket to get some juice and, um, came back.
And, uh, by the time I, you know, was like laying down ready to go to sleep, it was like maybe 10 or something.
I think maybe I was, or nine, I don't know, kind of, um, but then, uh, I kind of had insomnia, but I, I did this thing yesterday, this new thing where I listened to Montesquieu.
I just listened to the same book.
I did chapter 32, the last chapter, and I think it's the biggest, I haven't counted the chapters yet, but I think it's the biggest chapter of the whole book, 1200 pages.
And it's like a hundred page chapter or something. Cause it was going for like an hour. It seemed like, um, cause I just put it in my ears and I just laid down, you know, so it didn't stress me out.
One, one problem about insomnia is when you can't sleep, you can't, um, cause I don't really think I really slept last night.
I pretty much don't sleep. I get like maybe an hour and sometimes I'll go days and days and days, three or four days without even sleeping at all.

Um, but, um, I didn't sleep last night, but, um, the problem with sleep is when you get stressed and then you stress out about it and then you're just like, oh my God, you know, but if you're relaxed, then you can actually get 80% of your sleep.
Even if you don't get REM.
So, so yeah, I was just listening to page chapter 20, 32 on, um, Montesquieu.
And, um, you know, when I doubt does out, it would just be a background cause I didn't have it too loud.
I had it loud enough for people to hear, but not too loud to, um, you know, be in my face.
So, yeah, it was just kind of dozing, kind of pay attention, paying attention in and out, trying to sleep.
And then when I would get stressed about being awake, that's when you pay attention to the book some.
And then when you, when you're more relaxed, you don't need to pay attention to the book and try to sleep.
That's where you can sleep with, with, with a voice in the background.
And so, um, yeah, it was a pretty good technique.
I'm going to try that from now on.
Just listen to audio books in the background instead of sleep all night long.

Um, so I was thinking about last night, um, what I was going to do today.
I'm a little bit, I'm a little bit kind of worn, wearing myself out.
But I'm still not at the fitness yet, where I can do like six hours a day every single day for like three weeks straight, you know.
I can do six hours a day for like three days straight.
